# SecureEntry — Product Requirements Document

> **Project:** SecureEntry — Secure Authentication UI Platform
> **Version:** 1.0
> **Status:** Ready for Stakeholder Review
> **Prepared by:** Press, Documentation Agent — Forge Platform
> **Estimated Timeline:** 2 Weeks | **Complexity:** Low | **Est. Infrastructure Cost:** $5–25/month

---

## Executive Summary

Authentication is the first interaction a user has with any application — and yet it remains one of the most consistently neglected surfaces in product design. SecureEntry is a production-ready, secure, and accessible login page solution built on modern web standards, designed to eliminate the gap between what authentication flows *should* be and what they typically are in practice. It delivers a frictionless, branded, and fully accessible entry point that works beautifully across every device and screen size.

SecureEntry is engineered on a React 18 + TypeScript monolith architecture, combining Radix UI accessible primitives, React Hook Form with Zod schema validation, and a hardened Node.js backend with CSRF protection, Redis-backed session management, and OAuth 2.0 PKCE flows. Every design and architectural decision in this product traces directly back to a user need or a security requirement — nothing is included speculatively, and nothing critical has been left out. The result is a login experience that converts better, protects users more effectively, and gives development teams a clean, extensible foundation to build on.

For product owners and engineering teams tired of patching together authentication UI from disparate libraries, SecureEntry provides a coherent, well-documented, and fully themeable solution that can be deployed in two weeks and adapted to any brand identity. It meets WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards out of the box, targets a Lighthouse performance score of 90 or above, and is built to scale from startup to enterprise without architectural rework.

---

## The Problem

Modern web applications routinely lose users before they ever see the product — at the login screen. Poorly structured authentication forms, absent or cryptic error messages, no mobile optimization, and weak security patterns create a compounding problem that damages both conversion rates and user trust. Studies consistently show that login friction is a leading cause of abandonment in SaaS, internal tools, and consumer applications alike, yet authentication UI is rarely treated with the same design rigor as the rest of the product.

Beyond usability, login pages are high-value attack surfaces. Insufficient rate limiting, missing CSRF tokens, implicit OAuth flows, and no account lockout logic expose users and businesses to credential stuffing, brute-force attacks, and session hijacking. Most teams lack the time or specialized knowledge to implement all these protections correctly from scratch, resulting in security debt that accumulates silently until it becomes a breach.

For development teams, the absence of a standardized, accessible authentication component system forces repeated reinvention across projects. Every new application re-solves the same problems — form validation, password visibility toggling, OAuth integration, ARIA compliance — without any accumulated institutional knowledge. SecureEntry exists to solve this problem once, completely, and make the solution reusable.

---

## The Solution

SecureEntry addresses the authentication UX and security problem through a purpose-built, component-driven login interface backed by a hardened authentication service. Rather than a generic form template, SecureEntry is a complete authentication *system* — from the pixel-level detail of a password visibility toggle to the backend mechanics of Redis-backed rate limiting and session revocation.

On the frontend, Radix UI's WAI-ARIA compliant primitives eliminate the need to hand-roll accessible components, while React Hook Form minimizes re-renders for snappy perceived performance on mobile networks. Zod schemas provide type-safe validation shared across the client-server boundary, reducing an entire class of input-handling bugs. The OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow replaces the insecure implicit flow for all social login providers, and CSS custom properties combined with a Tailwind configuration file allow any team to fully rebrand the experience in minutes without touching component logic.

On the backend, a focused Node.js/Express monolith handles authentication, session management, MFA, and email — keeping the architecture simple and the timeline achievable. Redis provides sub-millisecond session lookups and rate limit counters. Vercel's edge CDN guarantees sub-2-second load times globally with automatic HTTPS and security headers. The net result is an authentication experience that is measurably faster, more accessible, more secure, and more maintainable than the status quo.

---

## User Personas

### Alex — End User (Returning SaaS Customer)

**Goals:**
- Log in quickly and without confusion to access the tools they pay for
- Recover account access independently when they forget their password
- Use preferred social login (Google) to avoid managing another password
- Have a consistent, trustworthy experience on both mobile and desktop

**Pain Points:**
- Login forms with no inline validation, forcing a full-page reload to learn of errors
- Password fields with no visibility toggle, causing repeated failed attempts on mobile
- Password reset flows that are confusing, broken, or send expired links
- Login pages that look nothing like the rest of the application, eroding trust

**How SecureEntry Helps:**
SecureEntry provides real-time inline validation so Alex knows immediately if their email format is wrong — before they submit. The password visibility toggle eliminates mis-typed password frustration on touchscreens. The branded, themeable interface matches the application Alex recognizes, and Google OAuth login means they may never need a password at all. Password reset is a guided, confirmation-backed flow with clearly communicated link expiry.

---

### Jordan — Product Owner / Business Stakeholder

**Goals:**
- Maximize user activation rates and minimize drop-off at the authentication stage
- Ensure the login page reflects brand identity and builds product credibility
- Demonstrate security compliance to enterprise customers and legal/compliance teams
- Ship a polished authentication experience without a multi-sprint investment

**Pain Points:**
- High bounce rates on the login page directly impacting Monthly Active User metrics
- Security audit findings related to weak authentication UI patterns
- Login page that looks like a generic template, undercutting brand investment elsewhere
- No visibility into authentication-stage drop-off or recovery flow completion

**How SecureEntry Helps:**
SecureEntry is designed to drive login success rates to 95%+, directly improving activation metrics Jordan is accountable for. The branding and theming system means the login page is an extension of the product, not an afterthought. Built-in security indicators, account lockout warnings, and CSRF protection provide documented evidence of security best practices for compliance conversations. The 2-week delivery timeline means Jordan gets a production-quality result without a long-cycle investment.

---

### Sam — Developer / UI Engineer

**Goals:**
- Implement a login page that passes accessibility audits without spending weeks on ARIA research
- Integrate authentication UI cleanly with existing REST or OAuth 2.0 backend APIs
- Work from a well-structured, component-based codebase with clear separation of concerns
- Ship confidently knowing the security fundamentals are handled correctly

**Pain Points:**
- No design system or component library standardized for authentication flows
- Spending significant time on accessibility compliance without clear guidance
- OAuth integration complexity and security pitfalls (implicit flow, token storage)
- Rebuilding the same login form across every new project from scratch

**How SecureEntry Helps:**
Radix UI primitives and the component architecture give Sam a fully accessible baseline — zero ARIA research required. The OAuth 2.0 PKCE implementation is pre-built and correct by default. RESTful API contracts are clearly defined and consistent. Zod schemas shared between client and server eliminate validation duplication. Sam inherits a codebase that is tested, typed, and documented — ready to integrate, extend, and hand off.

---

| Loading & Submission States | Spinner/progress indicator during API calls; disabled submit button during in-flight request; success and error state transitions | High | S |
| CSRF Protection & Secure Cookie Handling | csurf middleware on all auth endpoints; HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie attributes; token rotation on session creation | High | M |
| Rate Limiting & Account Lockout UI | Redis-backed rate limiter (express-rate-limit); lockout warning messaging after N failed attempts; CAPTCHA prompt trigger; user-facing feedback | High | M |

---

### Phase 2 — Post-MVP Enhancements

| Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) UI | OTP input screen (6-digit code) supporting authenticator app and SMS-based MFA; resend code flow; expiry countdown; integration with MFA/OTP service | Low | L |

---

## Technical Requirements

### Performance
- Page load time must be **under 2 seconds** on a standard 4G connection (target: ~1.2s with Vercel edge CDN)
- Lighthouse Performance score of **90 or above** in production build
- React Hook Form selected specifically to minimize re-renders during input — perceived input latency must be imperceptible
- All static assets (JS, CSS, fonts) must be code-split, compressed (Brotli/gzip), and cached at the CDN edge
- Redis session and rate limit lookups must complete in **under 5ms** (sub-millisecond typical for local Redis)

### Security
- **CSRF protection** via `csurf` middleware on all state-mutating auth endpoints (`POST /auth/login`, `POST /auth/reset-password`, etc.)
- **Secure cookies:** `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite=Strict` attributes enforced on all session cookies
- **OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow** mandatory for all social login providers — implicit flow explicitly prohibited
- **Bcrypt** (minimum cost factor 12) for all password hashing; plaintext passwords must never be logged or persisted
- **Rate limiting:** Maximum 5 failed login attempts per IP per 15-minute window before lockout/CAPTCHA trigger
- **HTTPS enforced** via Vercel automatic TLS + Cloudflare WAF layer; HSTS header with minimum 1-year max-age
- **JWT tokens** (if issued) must use RS256 signing; short-lived access tokens (15 min) with Redis-backed refresh token rotation
- **Session revocation** must be immediate and enforced via Redis — critical post-password-reset
- Input sanitization on all form fields server-side; no raw SQL queries (parameterized queries only)
- Security headers: `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`

### Accessibility
- Full compliance with **WCAG 2.1 Level AA** standards
- All form inputs must have explicit, associated `<label>` elements (not placeholder-only)
- Error messages must be announced via **ARIA live regions** (`aria-live="polite"` for inline, `assertive` for critical)
- Complete **keyboard navigation** support: Tab order logical, Enter submits form, Escape closes modals
- Visible **focus indicators** on all interactive elements (minimum 3px outline, not removed via `outline: none`)
- Color contrast minimum **4.5:1** for normal text, **3:1** for large text and UI components
- All icon-only buttons (e.g., password visibility toggle) must have accessible `aria-label`
- Zero critical violations in **Axe** and **Lighthouse** accessibility automated audits

### Scalability
- Monolith architecture is appropriate for current scope; auth service components (email, MFA, OAuth) are logically separated within the codebase to enable future microservice extraction without full rewrite
- Redis session store enables horizontal scaling of Node.js backend behind a load balancer without sticky sessions
- Stateless API design (except session cookie) ensures backend instances are interchangeable
- Database schema designed with indexing on `email`, `session_id`, and `created_at` to support high-frequency auth queries
- Estimated capacity: architecture supports **10,000+ concurrent sessions** without modification

### Browser & Platform Support
| Browser | Versions |
|---|---|
| Google Chrome | Latest 2 |
| Mozilla Firefox | Latest 2 |
| Apple Safari | Latest 2 |
| Microsoft Edge | Latest 2 |
| Mobile Safari (iOS) | iOS 15+ |
| Chrome for Android | Latest stable |

### Tech Stack Summary

| Primary Database | PostgreSQL 15 |
| Cache / Sessions | Redis 7 |
| Frontend Hosting | Vercel (edge CDN) |
| Backend Hosting | Railway or Render |
| DDoS / WAF | Cloudflare |
| CI/CD | GitHub Actions + Docker |

---

key_decisions.title: Monolith over Microservices
key_decisions.choice: Single backend service handling auth, session, MFA, and email
key_decisions.rationale: Given the low complexity rating and 2-week timeline, a monolith avoids distributed systems overhead. Auth logic is cohesive and does not benefit from service decomposition at this scale. Can be extracted later if needed.

key_decisions.title: React with Radix UI for Accessibility
key_decisions.choice: Radix UI unstyled primitives + Tailwind CSS for styling
key_decisions.rationale: Radix UI provides WAI-ARIA compliant, keyboard-navigable components out of the box (dialogs, checkboxes, labels), directly satisfying the WCAG 2.1 AA requirement without building from scratch, saving significant dev time.

key_decisions.title: OAuth 2.0 PKCE Flow for Social Login
key_decisions.choice: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) instead of implicit flow
key_decisions.rationale: PKCE is the current security best practice for SPAs with OAuth, preventing authorization code interception attacks. Supported by Google, GitHub, and Microsoft providers.

key_decisions.title: React Hook Form + Zod for Validation
key_decisions.rationale: React Hook Form minimizes re-renders during input (critical for perceived performance on mobile), while Zod provides type-safe validation schemas shared between frontend and backend, reducing bugs at the API boundary.

key_decisions.title: Redis for Session & Rate Limiting
key_decisions.choice: Redis-backed sessions instead of stateless JWT-only
key_decisions.rationale: Enables immediate session revocation (critical for security — e.g., after password reset), supports the Remember Me persistent session feature, and provides sub-millisecond rate limit counter lookups to prevent brute-force attacks.

key_decisions.title: Vercel Edge CDN for Frontend
key_decisions.choice: Vercel static hosting with global edge network
key_decisions.rationale: Guarantees sub-2-second page loads globally with zero config, aligns with the Lighthouse 90+ score requirement, and provides automatic HTTPS with proper security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options).

key_decisions.title: REST over GraphQL
key_decisions.choice: RESTful API with clearly scoped auth endpoints
key_decisions.rationale: Authentication flows map naturally to REST verbs (POST /auth/login, POST /auth/refresh, POST /auth/reset-password). GraphQL adds complexity with no benefit for this focused, low-complexity use case.

key_decisions.title: Component-Based Theming via CSS Custom Properties
key_decisions.choice: CSS variables + Tailwind config for brand theming
key_decisions.rationale: Allows Product Owners to swap logos, colors, and fonts via a single theme config file without touching component logic, satisfying the Branding & Theming Customization requirement with minimal architectural overhead.

# Work Orders

**Total Story Points:** 62 | **Sprints:** 2 | **Stories:** 18

---

## Epic 1: Core Authentication Form & Validation
> Deliver a fully functional, validated email/password login form with real-time feedback, loading states, and secure API integration.

### [E1-S1] Email & Password Login Form Component
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** frontend, react, forms

**User Story**
As an end user, I want a clean email and password login form so that I can quickly authenticate and access the application.

**Work Description**
Build the `AuthForm` React component using React Hook Form and Zod schema validation. The form must include email and password fields with controlled inputs, a submit button with a loading spinner state, and inline error display beneath each field on blur or submit. Wire the form to `POST /auth/login` via a typed `authApi` service module using Axios or fetch with proper error boundary handling.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Email field validates format (RFC 5322) inline on blur; password field requires minimum 8 characters
- [ ] Submit button displays a spinner and becomes disabled during the API request
- [ ] API error responses (401 invalid credentials, 429 rate-limited) surface as human-readable inline messages beneath the form, not browser alerts
- [ ] Successful login stores the session token in an HttpOnly cookie and redirects to the authenticated route
- [ ] Form state resets on navigation away without memory leaks (useEffect cleanup confirmed)

---

### [E1-S2] Password Visibility Toggle
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 2
**Tags:** frontend, accessibility, usability

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to toggle password visibility on the login form so that I can verify my input before submitting and reduce entry errors.

**Work Description**
Add a show/hide icon button inside the password input field using Radix UI's accessible button primitive. Toggle the `input[type]` between `"password"` and `"text"` on click. The icon should be an eye/eye-off SVG (sourced from Heroicons or Lucide) and must be keyboard-activatable and screen reader-labelled.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Icon button renders inside the right edge of the password input with no layout shift
- [ ] Clicking or pressing Enter/Space on the button toggles input type between `password` and `text`
- [ ] Button has `aria-label="Show password"` / `"Hide password"` that updates dynamically with state
- [ ] Toggle does not trigger form submission
- [ ] Password value is preserved across visibility toggling

---

### [E1-S3] Backend Login Endpoint with Rate Limiting & CSRF
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, security, api

**User Story**
As a product owner, I want login attempts to be rate-limited and CSRF-protected so that the application is not vulnerable to brute-force or cross-site request forgery attacks.

**Work Description**
Implement `POST /auth/login` in Express.js. Use `bcrypt.compare` for password verification against the PostgreSQL users table. Apply `express-rate-limit` middleware (max 10 attempts per IP per 15 minutes, stored in Redis). Issue session tokens as signed `jsonwebtoken` JWTs stored in Redis with a configurable TTL. Apply `csurf` middleware to all POST routes and return the CSRF token in a meta tag endpoint `GET /auth/csrf-token`.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Endpoint returns `200` with a session cookie on valid credentials; `401` with `{"error": "Invalid email or password"}` on failure (no field discrimination)
- [ ] After 10 failed attempts from the same IP within 15 minutes, endpoint returns `429` with a `Retry-After` header
- [ ] CSRF token is validated on every POST; missing or invalid token returns `403`
- [ ] Passwords are never logged; only sanitized audit events are written to the audit log table
- [ ] Redis session key is namespaced as `session:{userId}:{tokenId}` with TTL matching cookie expiry

---

### [E1-S4] Remember Me Persistent Session
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, backend, security

**User Story**
As a returning end user, I want to opt into a persistent login session so that I don't have to re-authenticate every time I close and reopen my browser.

**Work Description**
Add a "Remember me" Radix UI Checkbox component to the `AuthForm`. When checked, pass a `rememberMe: true` flag in the login request body. On the backend, conditionally set the session JWT TTL to 30 days (vs. the default 1-hour session TTL) and set the cookie `Max-Age` accordingly. Ensure the Redis session key TTL matches the cookie expiry to prevent zombie sessions.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Checkbox renders with a visible label "Remember me for 30 days" and is keyboard-navigable
- [ ] Unchecked: session cookie is a browser-session cookie (no `Max-Age`); JWT TTL is 1 hour in Redis
- [ ] Checked: cookie has `Max-Age=2592000` (30 days); Redis session TTL is 30 days
- [ ] Checking "Remember me" does not alter the login flow or add latency perceptible to the user
- [ ] Persistent sessions are invalidated in Redis immediately upon explicit logout

---

### [E1-S5] Account Lockout Warning UI & Security Feedback
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, security, ux

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to see clear feedback when my account is temporarily locked due to failed attempts so that I understand what happened and how to recover.

**Work Description**
Handle the `429` rate-limit response in the `AuthForm` component by rendering a dismissible warning banner (using Radix UI's `Alert` pattern) above the form. Display the lockout duration parsed from the `Retry-After` response header (e.g., "Too many attempts. Try again in 12 minutes."). Disable the submit button and countdown in real time using a `setInterval` hook until the lockout window expires.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] A yellow/amber warning banner appears above the form on `429` response with a human-readable countdown
- [ ] Submit button is disabled and shows remaining lockout time (e.g., "Locked — 11:43 remaining") updated every second
- [ ] Banner has `role="alert"` so screen readers announce it immediately
- [ ] Countdown reaches zero, re-enables the form, and dismisses the banner without a page reload
- [ ] Warning banner is dismissible via a close button that does not re-enable the locked form prematurely

---

## Epic 2: Account Recovery & Password Reset Flow
> Enable users to securely recover access to their accounts via an email-based password reset flow with expiry-aware tokens.

### [E2-S1] Forgot Password Link & Request Screen
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, ux

**User Story**
As an end user, I want a clearly visible "Forgot password?" link on the login form so that I can initiate account recovery without confusion.

**Work Description**
Add a "Forgot password?" anchor link below the password field in `AuthForm`. Clicking it renders a separate `ForgotPasswordForm` component (route: `/auth/forgot-password`) with a single email input field and a submit button. On submission, call `POST /auth/password/request-reset`. Display a generic success message ("If this email is registered, you'll receive a reset link") regardless of whether the email exists, to prevent user enumeration.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Link is visually distinct, focusable via keyboard Tab, and has descriptive `aria-label="Forgot your password? Reset it here"`
- [ ] `/auth/forgot-password` renders the recovery form with email validation matching the main form's Zod schema
- [ ] Submitting always shows the same success message whether email exists or not (anti-enumeration)
- [ ] Form shows a loading state during the API call and prevents duplicate submissions
- [ ] A "Back to login" link is present and returns to `/auth/login` without browser history pollution

---

### [E2-S2] Password Reset Email Backend
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, email, security

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to receive a secure, time-limited password reset email so that I can regain access to my account safely.

**Work Description**
Implement `POST /auth/password/request-reset` in Express.js. If the email is found in PostgreSQL, generate a cryptographically random reset token (using `crypto.randomBytes(32)`), store its SHA-256 hash in the `password_reset_tokens` table with a 1-hour expiry, and send a reset email via Nodemailer containing a link to `{APP_URL}/auth/reset-password?token={rawToken}`. Token lookup must compare the hash of the provided token, never the raw value.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Reset token is 32 random bytes, hex-encoded; only its SHA-256 hash is persisted in the database
- [ ] Token expires after 60 minutes; `expires_at` column is checked on use and the token is deleted after first use
- [ ] Email is sent with a plain-text and HTML multipart body including the reset link and a note that the link expires in 1 hour
- [ ] Endpoint always returns `200` regardless of email existence (anti-enumeration); errors are only logged server-side
- [ ] Rate limit on this endpoint: max 3 requests per email per hour, tracked in Redis

---

### [E2-S3] Password Reset Form & Confirmation Screen
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 4
**Tags:** frontend, backend, security

**User Story**
As an end user, I want a secure form to set a new password using my reset link so that I can regain access to my account with a new credential.

**Work Description**
Build `ResetPasswordForm` component at `/auth/reset-password?token=`. On mount, validate the token is present in the URL; if absent, redirect to `/auth/forgot-password`. The form collects a new password and confirm password, validated via Zod (min 8 chars, must match). On submit, call `POST /auth/password/reset` with the token and new password. On success, show a confirmation screen with a "Back to login" CTA. On expired/invalid token, show an actionable error with a link to re-request a reset.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Token is extracted from the URL query parameter and included in the request body (never exposed in logs)
- [ ] "New password" and "Confirm password" fields validate match in real time with Zod `refine`; mismatched passwords block submission
- [ ] `POST /auth/password/reset` verifies the token hash, updates the bcrypt password hash, deletes the used token, and invalidates all active Redis sessions for that user
- [ ] Expired or already-used tokens return a clear error: "This reset link has expired or already been used. Request a new one." with a link to `/auth/forgot-password`
- [ ] Password reset completion rate is trackable via a server-side audit log event `password_reset_completed`

---

### [E2-S4] Password Reset — Backend Endpoint
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** backend, security, api

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a secure and auditable password reset endpoint so that the reset flow is protected against token reuse and session fixation.

**Work Description**
Implement `POST /auth/password/reset` accepting `{ token, newPassword }`. Validate the SHA-256 hash of the received token against the `password_reset_tokens` table, checking `expires_at > NOW()` and `used = false`. Hash the new password with `bcrypt` (cost factor 12), update the `users` table, mark the token as used (or delete it), and flush all Redis sessions for `userId` using `DEL session:{userId}:*` pattern to force re-authentication everywhere.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Endpoint returns `200` on success; `400` with `{"error": "Invalid or expired token"}` for bad/expired tokens — same message for both (no discrimination)
- [ ] New password is hashed with `bcrypt` cost factor 12 before storage; plaintext is never logged
- [ ] All Redis session keys for the affected user are invalidated synchronously before the `200` response is returned
- [ ] Token row is deleted (not just flagged) from `password_reset_tokens` after successful use
- [ ] Audit log records `password_reset_completed` with `userId`, `timestamp`, and `ip` — no token value

---

## Epic 3: OAuth Social Login & MFA Support
> Integrate third-party OAuth providers for one-click sign-in and build the MFA OTP verification UI for enhanced security.

### [E3-S1] OAuth Provider Buttons UI
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, oauth, ux

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to sign in with Google, GitHub, or Microsoft so that I can authenticate quickly without managing a separate password.

**Work Description**
Build the `OAuthButtons` component rendering branded buttons for Google, GitHub, and Microsoft using their official brand assets (SVG icons). Each button initiates the OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow by redirecting to `GET /auth/oauth/{provider}`. Display provider buttons in a visually grouped section below the email/password form, separated by an "or continue with" divider. Buttons must be individually focusable and labelled for screen readers.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Three buttons render for Google, GitHub, and Microsoft with correct brand colors and logos per each provider's brand guidelines
- [ ] Each button has `aria-label="Sign in with {Provider}"` and is keyboard focusable
- [ ] Clicking a button redirects to `/auth/oauth/{provider}` which initiates the server-side PKCE redirect
- [ ] A loading state is applied to the clicked button (spinner, disabled) to prevent double-clicks during redirect
- [ ] Buttons are hidden/disabled gracefully if a provider is not configured server-side (feature-flag via env variable)

---

### [E3-S2] OAuth 2.0 PKCE Backend Integration
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, oauth, security

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a secure OAuth 2.0 PKCE callback handler so that social login tokens are exchanged server-side and not exposed to the browser.

**Work Description**
Configure Passport.js with Google, GitHub, and Microsoft OAuth strategies. `GET /auth/oauth/:provider` generates a PKCE `code_verifier`/`code_challenge` pair, stores the verifier in a short-lived Redis key (`pkce:{state}`, TTL 5 min), and redirects to the provider's authorization URL. `GET /auth/oauth/:provider/callback` validates the `state` parameter, retrieves the verifier from Redis, exchanges the code for tokens, upserts the user in PostgreSQL (`oauth_accounts` table linked to `users`), creates a session in Redis, and sets the session cookie before redirecting to the app.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] PKCE `code_verifier` is 43–128 chars of `[A-Za-z0-9\-._~]`; `code_challenge` is its S256 hash; both match the provider's requirements
- [ ] `state` parameter is validated on callback; mismatched or missing state returns `400` and aborts the flow
- [ ] OAuth user upsert correctly handles: new user (create), existing email (link account), returning OAuth user (update tokens)
- [ ] Provider access tokens are never stored in PostgreSQL; only `provider_user_id` and `provider` are persisted
- [ ] All three providers (Google, GitHub, Microsoft) complete the full auth cycle in the local dev environment using test app credentials

---

### [E3-S3] MFA OTP Verification Screen
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** low | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 4
**Tags:** frontend, mfa, security

**User Story**
As a security-conscious end user, I want to enter a one-time password as a second authentication step so that my account is protected even if my password is compromised.

**Work Description**
Build the `MFAVerificationScreen` component that renders after a successful primary login when `mfa_required: true` is returned in the login response

# SecureEntry — Product Requirements Document

> **Project:** SecureEntry — Secure Authentication UI Platform
> **Version:** 1.0
> **Status:** Ready for Stakeholder Review
> **Prepared by:** Press, Documentation Agent — Forge Platform
> **Estimated Timeline:** 2 Weeks | **Complexity:** Low | **Est. Infrastructure Cost:** $5–25/month

---

## Executive Summary

Authentication is the first interaction a user has with any application — and yet it remains one of the most consistently neglected surfaces in product design. SecureEntry is a production-ready, secure, and accessible login page solution built on modern web standards, designed to eliminate the gap between what authentication flows *should* be and what they typically are in practice. It delivers a frictionless, branded, and fully accessible entry point that works beautifully across every device and screen size.

SecureEntry is engineered on a React 18 + TypeScript monolith architecture, combining Radix UI accessible primitives, React Hook Form with Zod schema validation, and a hardened Node.js backend with CSRF protection, Redis-backed session management, and OAuth 2.0 PKCE flows. Every design and architectural decision in this product traces directly back to a user need or a security requirement — nothing is included speculatively, and nothing critical has been left out. The result is a login experience that converts better, protects users more effectively, and gives development teams a clean, extensible foundation to build on.

For product owners and engineering teams tired of patching together authentication UI from disparate libraries, SecureEntry provides a coherent, well-documented, and fully themeable solution that can be deployed in two weeks and adapted to any brand identity. It meets WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards out of the box, targets a Lighthouse performance score of 90 or above, and is built to scale from startup to enterprise without architectural rework.

---

## The Problem

Modern web applications routinely lose users before they ever see the product — at the login screen. Poorly structured authentication forms, absent or cryptic error messages, no mobile optimization, and weak security patterns create a compounding problem that damages both conversion rates and user trust. Studies consistently show that login friction is a leading cause of abandonment in SaaS, internal tools, and consumer applications alike, yet authentication UI is rarely treated with the same design rigor as the rest of the product.

Beyond usability, login pages are high-value attack surfaces. Insufficient rate limiting, missing CSRF tokens, implicit OAuth flows, and no account lockout logic expose users and businesses to credential stuffing, brute-force attacks, and session hijacking. Most teams lack the time or specialized knowledge to implement all these protections correctly from scratch, resulting in security debt that accumulates silently until it becomes a breach.

For development teams, the absence of a standardized, accessible authentication component system forces repeated reinvention across projects. Every new application re-solves the same problems — form validation, password visibility toggling, OAuth integration, ARIA compliance — without any accumulated institutional knowledge. SecureEntry exists to solve this problem once, completely, and make the solution reusable.

---

## The Solution

SecureEntry addresses the authentication UX and security problem through a purpose-built, component-driven login interface backed by a hardened authentication service. Rather than a generic form template, SecureEntry is a complete authentication *system* — from the pixel-level detail of a password visibility toggle to the backend mechanics of Redis-backed rate limiting and session revocation.

On the frontend, Radix UI's WAI-ARIA compliant primitives eliminate the need to hand-roll accessible components, while React Hook Form minimizes re-renders for snappy perceived performance on mobile networks. Zod schemas provide type-safe validation shared across the client-server boundary, reducing an entire class of input-handling bugs. The OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow replaces the insecure implicit flow for all social login providers, and CSS custom properties combined with a Tailwind configuration file allow any team to fully rebrand the experience in minutes without touching component logic.

On the backend, a focused Node.js/Express monolith handles authentication, session management, MFA, and email — keeping the architecture simple and the timeline achievable. Redis provides sub-millisecond session lookups and rate limit counters. Vercel's edge CDN guarantees sub-2-second load times globally with automatic HTTPS and security headers. The net result is an authentication experience that is measurably faster, more accessible, more secure, and more maintainable than the status quo.

---

## User Personas

### Alex — End User (Returning SaaS Customer)

**Goals:**
- Log in quickly and without confusion to access the tools they pay for
- Recover account access independently when they forget their password
- Use preferred social login (Google) to avoid managing another password
- Have a consistent, trustworthy experience on both mobile and desktop

**Pain Points:**
- Login forms with no inline validation, forcing a full-page reload to learn of errors
- Password fields with no visibility toggle, causing repeated failed attempts on mobile
- Password reset flows that are confusing, broken, or send expired links
- Login pages that look nothing like the rest of the application, eroding trust

**How SecureEntry Helps:**
SecureEntry provides real-time inline validation so Alex knows immediately if their email format is wrong — before they submit. The password visibility toggle eliminates mis-typed password frustration on touchscreens. The branded, themeable interface matches the application Alex recognizes, and Google OAuth login means they may never need a password at all. Password reset is a guided, confirmation-backed flow with clearly communicated link expiry.

---

### Jordan — Product Owner / Business Stakeholder

**Goals:**
- Maximize user activation rates and minimize drop-off at the authentication stage
- Ensure the login page reflects brand identity and builds product credibility
- Demonstrate security compliance to enterprise customers and legal/compliance teams
- Ship a polished authentication experience without a multi-sprint investment

**Pain Points:**
- High bounce rates on the login page directly impacting Monthly Active User metrics
- Security audit findings related to weak authentication UI patterns
- Login page that looks like a generic template, undercutting brand investment elsewhere
- No visibility into authentication-stage drop-off or recovery flow completion

**How SecureEntry Helps:**
SecureEntry is designed to drive login success rates to 95%+, directly improving activation metrics Jordan is accountable for. The branding and theming system means the login page is an extension of the product, not an afterthought. Built-in security indicators, account lockout warnings, and CSRF protection provide documented evidence of security best practices for compliance conversations. The 2-week delivery timeline means Jordan gets a production-quality result without a long-cycle investment.

---

### Sam — Developer / UI Engineer

**Goals:**
- Implement a login page that passes accessibility audits without spending weeks on ARIA research
- Integrate authentication UI cleanly with existing REST or OAuth 2.0 backend APIs
- Work from a well-structured, component-based codebase with clear separation of concerns
- Ship confidently knowing the security fundamentals are handled correctly

**Pain Points:**
- No design system or component library standardized for authentication flows
- Spending significant time on accessibility compliance without clear guidance
- OAuth integration complexity and security pitfalls (implicit flow, token storage)
- Rebuilding the same login form across every new project from scratch

**How SecureEntry Helps:**
Radix UI primitives and the component architecture give Sam a fully accessible baseline — zero ARIA research required. The OAuth 2.0 PKCE implementation is pre-built and correct by default. RESTful API contracts are clearly defined and consistent. Zod schemas shared between client and server eliminate validation duplication. Sam inherits a codebase that is tested, typed, and documented — ready to integrate, extend, and hand off.

---

## Feature Requirements

### MVP — Phase 1

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Email & Password Authentication Form | Clean, validated login form with email/password fields, real-time inline validation (Zod + React Hook Form), loading states, and clear error messaging on failed submission | High | S |
| Password Visibility Toggle | Show/hide password input button with accessible label, icon feedback, and touch-friendly tap target for mobile devices | High | S |
| Forgot Password / Account Recovery Flow | Recovery link on login form → email input screen → confirmation screen → expiry-aware reset link → new password screen with success confirmation | High | M |
| Social / OAuth Login (Google, GitHub, Microsoft) | OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow integration with at minimum Google; GitHub and Microsoft as additional providers; consistent button styling and error handling | High | M |
| Responsive & Mobile-First Layout | Fluid layout adapting across mobile (320px+), tablet, and desktop breakpoints; touch-friendly inputs (min 44px tap targets); appropriate font scaling | High | S |
| Client-Side Form Validation | Zod schema-driven validation with real-time field-level feedback; email format, password minimum requirements, required field enforcement | High | S |
| Loading & Submission States | Spinner/progress indicator during API calls; disabled submit button during in-flight request; success and error state transitions | High | S |
| CSRF Protection & Secure Cookie Handling | csurf middleware on all auth endpoints; HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie attributes; token rotation on session creation | High | M |
| Rate Limiting & Account Lockout UI | Redis-backed rate limiter (express-rate-limit); lockout warning messaging after N failed attempts; CAPTCHA prompt trigger; user-facing feedback | High | M |
| Accessibility Compliance (WCAG 2.1 AA) | Proper label/input associations; keyboard navigation and visible focus indicators; ARIA roles and live regions for errors; 4.5:1 color contrast minimum; screen reader testing | Medium | M |
| Remember Me / Persistent Session | Checkbox opting into extended session TTL; Redis session with appropriate expiry; clear UX copy about what persistence means | Medium | S |
| Branding & Theming Customization | Logo slot, primary color, font family, and border radius configurable via CSS custom properties + Tailwind config; no component logic changes required | Medium | S |
| Security Indicators | HTTPS lock visual cue; account lockout warning copy; rate limit feedback messaging | Medium | S |

---

### Phase 2 — Post-MVP Enhancements

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) UI | OTP input screen (6-digit code) supporting authenticator app and SMS-based MFA; resend code flow; expiry countdown; integration with MFA/OTP service | Low | L |
| Passkey / WebAuthn Support | Passwordless login via device biometrics or hardware security keys using the WebAuthn API; "Sign in with passkey" button on login form | Low | L |
| Login Activity & Anomaly Alerts | Email notification to user on new device/location login; audit log UI in account settings; suspicious activity flag | Low | M |
| Internationalization (i18n) | Full translation support for all UI strings; RTL layout support; locale-based input format handling | Low | M |
| Admin Theming Dashboard | GUI-based theme editor for product owners to configure branding without touching config files; live preview | Low | L |
| SSO / SAML 2.0 Support | Enterprise single sign-on via SAML 2.0 for B2B customers requiring IdP integration (Okta, Azure AD, OneLogin) | Low | XL |
| Login Analytics Dashboard | Drop-off funnel visualization, OAuth vs. password split, recovery flow completion rates, geographic login map | Low | L |

---

## Technical Requirements

### Performance
- Page load time must be **under 2 seconds** on a standard 4G connection (target: ~1.2s with Vercel edge CDN)
- Lighthouse Performance score of **90 or above** in production build
- React Hook Form selected specifically to minimize re-renders during input — perceived input latency must be imperceptible
- All static assets (JS, CSS, fonts) must be code-split, compressed (Brotli/gzip), and cached at the CDN edge
- Redis session and rate limit lookups must complete in **under 5ms** (sub-millisecond typical for local Redis)

### Security
- **CSRF protection** via `csurf` middleware on all state-mutating auth endpoints (`POST /auth/login`, `POST /auth/reset-password`, etc.)
- **Secure cookies:** `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite=Strict` attributes enforced on all session cookies
- **OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow** mandatory for all social login providers — implicit flow explicitly prohibited
- **Bcrypt** (minimum cost factor 12) for all password hashing; plaintext passwords must never be logged or persisted
- **Rate limiting:** Maximum 5 failed login attempts per IP per 15-minute window before lockout/CAPTCHA trigger
- **HTTPS enforced** via Vercel automatic TLS + Cloudflare WAF layer; HSTS header with minimum 1-year max-age
- **JWT tokens** (if issued) must use RS256 signing; short-lived access tokens (15 min) with Redis-backed refresh token rotation
- **Session revocation** must be immediate and enforced via Redis — critical post-password-reset
- Input sanitization on all form fields server-side; no raw SQL queries (parameterized queries only)
- Security headers: `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`

### Accessibility
- Full compliance with **WCAG 2.1 Level AA** standards
- All form inputs must have explicit, associated `<label>` elements (not placeholder-only)
- Error messages must be announced via **ARIA live regions** (`aria-live="polite"` for inline, `assertive` for critical)
- Complete **keyboard navigation** support: Tab order logical, Enter submits form, Escape closes modals
- Visible **focus indicators** on all interactive elements (minimum 3px outline, not removed via `outline: none`)
- Color contrast minimum **4.5:1** for normal text, **3:1** for large text and UI components
- All icon-only buttons (e.g., password visibility toggle) must have accessible `aria-label`
- Zero critical violations in **Axe** and **Lighthouse** accessibility automated audits

### Scalability
- Monolith architecture is appropriate for current scope; auth service components (email, MFA, OAuth) are logically separated within the codebase to enable future microservice extraction without full rewrite
- Redis session store enables horizontal scaling of Node.js backend behind a load balancer without sticky sessions
- Stateless API design (except session cookie) ensures backend instances are interchangeable
- Database schema designed with indexing on `email`, `session_id`, and `created_at` to support high-frequency auth queries
- Estimated capacity: architecture supports **10,000+ concurrent sessions** without modification

### Browser & Platform Support
| Browser | Versions |
|---|---|
| Google Chrome | Latest 2 |
| Mozilla Firefox | Latest 2 |
| Apple Safari | Latest 2 |
| Microsoft Edge | Latest 2 |
| Mobile Safari (iOS) | iOS 15+ |
| Chrome for Android | Latest stable |

### Tech Stack Summary
| Layer | Technology |

| Frontend Framework | React 18 + TypeScript |
| Form Management | React Hook Form + Zod |
| UI Primitives | Radix UI |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS |
| Animations | Framer Motion |
| Build Tool | Vite |
| Backend Runtime | Node.js 20 + Express.js |
| Auth Middleware | Passport.js (OAuth strategies) |
| Security | express-rate-limit, csurf, bcrypt, jsonwebtoken |
| Email | Nodemailer |
| Primary Database | PostgreSQL 15 |
| Cache / Sessions | Redis 7 |
| Frontend Hosting | Vercel (edge CDN) |
| Backend Hosting | Railway or Render |
| DDoS / WAF | Cloudflare |
| CI/CD | GitHub Actions + Docker |

---

key_decisions.title: Monolith over Microservices
key_decisions.choice: Single backend service handling auth, session, MFA, and email
key_decisions.rationale: Given the low complexity rating and 2-week timeline, a monolith avoids distributed systems overhead. Auth logic is cohesive and does not benefit from service decomposition at this scale. Can be extracted later if needed.

key_decisions.title: React with Radix UI for Accessibility
key_decisions.choice: Radix UI unstyled primitives + Tailwind CSS for styling
key_decisions.rationale: Radix UI provides WAI-ARIA compliant, keyboard-navigable components out of the box (dialogs, checkboxes, labels), directly satisfying the WCAG 2.1 AA requirement without building from scratch, saving significant dev time.

key_decisions.title: OAuth 2.0 PKCE Flow for Social Login
key_decisions.choice: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) instead of implicit flow
key_decisions.rationale: PKCE is the current security best practice for SPAs with OAuth, preventing authorization code interception attacks. Supported by Google, GitHub, and Microsoft providers.

key_decisions.title: React Hook Form + Zod for Validation
key_decisions.rationale: React Hook Form minimizes re-renders during input (critical for perceived performance on mobile), while Zod provides type-safe validation schemas shared between frontend and backend, reducing bugs at the API boundary.

key_decisions.title: Redis for Session & Rate Limiting
key_decisions.choice: Redis-backed sessions instead of stateless JWT-only
key_decisions.rationale: Enables immediate session revocation (critical for security — e.g., after password reset), supports the Remember Me persistent session feature, and provides sub-millisecond rate limit counter lookups to prevent brute-force attacks.

key_decisions.title: Vercel Edge CDN for Frontend
key_decisions.choice: Vercel static hosting with global edge network
key_decisions.rationale: Guarantees sub-2-second page loads globally with zero config, aligns with the Lighthouse 90+ score requirement, and provides automatic HTTPS with proper security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options).

key_decisions.title: REST over GraphQL
key_decisions.choice: RESTful API with clearly scoped auth endpoints
key_decisions.rationale: Authentication flows map naturally to REST verbs (POST /auth/login, POST /auth/refresh, POST /auth/reset-password). GraphQL adds complexity with no benefit for this focused, low-complexity use case.

key_decisions.title: Component-Based Theming via CSS Custom Properties
key_decisions.choice: CSS variables + Tailwind config for brand theming
key_decisions.rationale: Allows Product Owners to swap logos, colors, and fonts via a single theme config file without touching component logic, satisfying the Branding & Theming Customization requirement with minimal architectural overhead.

# Work Orders

**Total Story Points:** 62 | **Sprints:** 2 | **Stories:** 18

---

## Epic 1: Core Authentication Form & Validation
> Deliver a fully functional, validated email/password login form with real-time feedback, loading states, and secure API integration.

### [E1-S1] Email & Password Login Form Component
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** frontend, react, forms

**User Story**
As an end user, I want a clean email and password login form so that I can quickly authenticate and access the application.

**Work Description**
Build the `AuthForm` React component using React Hook Form and Zod schema validation. The form must include email and password fields with controlled inputs, a submit button with a loading spinner state, and inline error display beneath each field on blur or submit. Wire the form to `POST /auth/login` via a typed `authApi` service module using Axios or fetch with proper error boundary handling.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Email field validates format (RFC 5322) inline on blur; password field requires minimum 8 characters
- [ ] Submit button displays a spinner and becomes disabled during the API request
- [ ] API error responses (401 invalid credentials, 429 rate-limited) surface as human-readable inline messages beneath the form, not browser alerts
- [ ] Successful login stores the session token in an HttpOnly cookie and redirects to the authenticated route
- [ ] Form state resets on navigation away without memory leaks (useEffect cleanup confirmed)

---

### [E1-S2] Password Visibility Toggle
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 2
**Tags:** frontend, accessibility, usability

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to toggle password visibility on the login form so that I can verify my input before submitting and reduce entry errors.

**Work Description**
Add a show/hide icon button inside the password input field using Radix UI's accessible button primitive. Toggle the `input[type]` between `"password"` and `"text"` on click. The icon should be an eye/eye-off SVG (sourced from Heroicons or Lucide) and must be keyboard-activatable and screen reader-labelled.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Icon button renders inside the right edge of the password input with no layout shift
- [ ] Clicking or pressing Enter/Space on the button toggles input type between `password` and `text`
- [ ] Button has `aria-label="Show password"` / `"Hide password"` that updates dynamically with state
- [ ] Toggle does not trigger form submission
- [ ] Password value is preserved across visibility toggling

---

### [E1-S3] Backend Login Endpoint with Rate Limiting & CSRF
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, security, api

**User Story**
As a product owner, I want login attempts to be rate-limited and CSRF-protected so that the application is not vulnerable to brute-force or cross-site request forgery attacks.

**Work Description**
Implement `POST /auth/login` in Express.js. Use `bcrypt.compare` for password verification against the PostgreSQL users table. Apply `express-rate-limit` middleware (max 10 attempts per IP per 15 minutes, stored in Redis). Issue session tokens as signed `jsonwebtoken` JWTs stored in Redis with a configurable TTL. Apply `csurf` middleware to all POST routes and return the CSRF token in a meta tag endpoint `GET /auth/csrf-token`.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Endpoint returns `200` with a session cookie on valid credentials; `401` with `{"error": "Invalid email or password"}` on failure (no field discrimination)
- [ ] After 10 failed attempts from the same IP within 15 minutes, endpoint returns `429` with a `Retry-After` header
- [ ] CSRF token is validated on every POST; missing or invalid token returns `403`
- [ ] Passwords are never logged; only sanitized audit events are written to the audit log table
- [ ] Redis session key is namespaced as `session:{userId}:{tokenId}` with TTL matching cookie expiry

---

### [E1-S4] Remember Me Persistent Session
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, backend, security

**User Story**
As a returning end user, I want to opt into a persistent login session so that I don't have to re-authenticate every time I close and reopen my browser.

**Work Description**
Add a "Remember me" Radix UI Checkbox component to the `AuthForm`. When checked, pass a `rememberMe: true` flag in the login request body. On the backend, conditionally set the session JWT TTL to 30 days (vs. the default 1-hour session TTL) and set the cookie `Max-Age` accordingly. Ensure the Redis session key TTL matches the cookie expiry to prevent zombie sessions.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Checkbox renders with a visible label "Remember me for 30 days" and is keyboard-navigable
- [ ] Unchecked: session cookie is a browser-session cookie (no `Max-Age`); JWT TTL is 1 hour in Redis
- [ ] Checked: cookie has `Max-Age=2592000` (30 days); Redis session TTL is 30 days
- [ ] Checking "Remember me" does not alter the login flow or add latency perceptible to the user
- [ ] Persistent sessions are invalidated in Redis immediately upon explicit logout

---

### [E1-S5] Account Lockout Warning UI & Security Feedback
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, security, ux

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to see clear feedback when my account is temporarily locked due to failed attempts so that I understand what happened and how to recover.

**Work Description**
Handle the `429` rate-limit response in the `AuthForm` component by rendering a dismissible warning banner (using Radix UI's `Alert` pattern) above the form. Display the lockout duration parsed from the `Retry-After` response header (e.g., "Too many attempts. Try again in 12 minutes."). Disable the submit button and countdown in real time using a `setInterval` hook until the lockout window expires.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] A yellow/amber warning banner appears above the form on `429` response with a human-readable countdown
- [ ] Submit button is disabled and shows remaining lockout time (e.g., "Locked — 11:43 remaining") updated every second
- [ ] Banner has `role="alert"` so screen readers announce it immediately
- [ ] Countdown reaches zero, re-enables the form, and dismisses the banner without a page reload
- [ ] Warning banner is dismissible via a close button that does not re-enable the locked form prematurely

## Epic 2: Account Recovery & Password Reset Flow
> Enable users to securely recover access to their accounts via an email-based password reset flow with expiry-aware tokens.

### [E2-S1] Forgot Password Link & Request Screen
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, ux

**User Story**
As an end user, I want a clearly visible "Forgot password?" link on the login form so that I can initiate account recovery without confusion.

**Work Description**
Add a "Forgot password?" anchor link below the password field in `AuthForm`. Clicking it renders a separate `ForgotPasswordForm` component (route: `/auth/forgot-password`) with a single email input field and a submit button. On submission, call `POST /auth/password/request-reset`. Display a generic success message ("If this email is registered, you'll receive a reset link") regardless of whether the email exists, to prevent user enumeration.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Link is visually distinct, focusable via keyboard Tab, and has descriptive `aria-label="Forgot your password? Reset it here"`
- [ ] `/auth/forgot-password` renders the recovery form with email validation matching the main form's Zod schema
- [ ] Submitting always shows the same success message whether email exists or not (anti-enumeration)
- [ ] Form shows a loading state during the API call and prevents duplicate submissions
- [ ] A "Back to login" link is present and returns to `/auth/login` without browser history pollution

---

### [E2-S2] Password Reset Email Backend
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, email, security

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to receive a secure, time-limited password reset email so that I can regain access to my account safely.

**Work Description**
Implement `POST /auth/password/request-reset` in Express.js. If the email is found in PostgreSQL, generate a cryptographically random reset token (using `crypto.randomBytes(32)`), store its SHA-256 hash in the `password_reset_tokens` table with a 1-hour expiry, and send a reset email via Nodemailer containing a link to `{APP_URL}/auth/reset-password?token={rawToken}`. Token lookup must compare the hash of the provided token, never the raw value.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Reset token is 32 random bytes, hex-encoded; only its SHA-256 hash is persisted in the database
- [ ] Token expires after 60 minutes; `expires_at` column is checked on use and the token is deleted after first use
- [ ] Email is sent with a plain-text and HTML multipart body including the reset link and a note that the link expires in 1 hour
- [ ] Endpoint always returns `200` regardless of email existence (anti-enumeration); errors are only logged server-side
- [ ] Rate limit on this endpoint: max 3 requests per email per hour, tracked in Redis

---

### [E2-S3] Password Reset Form & Confirmation Screen
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 4
**Tags:** frontend, backend, security

**User Story**
As an end user, I want a secure form to set a new password using my reset link so that I can regain access to my account with a new credential.

**Work Description**
Build `ResetPasswordForm` component at `/auth/reset-password?token=`. On mount, validate the token is present in the URL; if absent, redirect to `/auth/forgot-password`. The form collects a new password and confirm password, validated via Zod (min 8 chars, must match). On submit, call `POST /auth/password/reset` with the token and new password. On success, show a confirmation screen with a "Back to login" CTA. On expired/invalid token, show an actionable error with a link to re-request a reset.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Token is extracted from the URL query parameter and included in the request body (never exposed in logs)
- [ ] "New password" and "Confirm password" fields validate match in real time with Zod `refine`; mismatched passwords block submission
- [ ] `POST /auth/password/reset` verifies the token hash, updates the bcrypt password hash, deletes the used token, and invalidates all active Redis sessions for that user
- [ ] Expired or already-used tokens return a clear error: "This reset link has expired or already been used. Request a new one." with a link to `/auth/forgot-password`
- [ ] Password reset completion rate is trackable via a server-side audit log event `password_reset_completed`

---

### [E2-S4] Password Reset — Backend Endpoint
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** backend, security, api

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a secure and auditable password reset endpoint so that the reset flow is protected against token reuse and session fixation.

**Work Description**
Implement `POST /auth/password/reset` accepting `{ token, newPassword }`. Validate the SHA-256 hash of the received token against the `password_reset_tokens` table, checking `expires_at > NOW()` and `used = false`. Hash the new password with `bcrypt` (cost factor 12), update the `users` table, mark the token as used (or delete it), and flush all Redis sessions for `userId` using `DEL session:{userId}:*` pattern to force re-authentication everywhere.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Endpoint returns `200` on success; `400` with `{"error": "Invalid or expired token"}` for bad/expired tokens — same message for both (no discrimination)
- [ ] New password is hashed with `bcrypt` cost factor 12 before storage; plaintext is never logged
- [ ] All Redis session keys for the affected user are invalidated synchronously before the `200` response is returned
- [ ] Token row is deleted (not just flagged) from `password_reset_tokens` after successful use
- [ ] Audit log records `password_reset_completed` with `userId`, `timestamp`, and `ip` — no token value

---

## Epic 3: OAuth Social Login & MFA Support
> Integrate third-party OAuth providers for one-click sign-in and build the MFA OTP verification UI for enhanced security.

### [E3-S1] OAuth Provider Buttons UI
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 3
**Tags:** frontend, oauth, ux

**User Story**
As an end user, I want to sign in with Google, GitHub, or Microsoft so that I can authenticate quickly without managing a separate password.

**Work Description**
Build the `OAuthButtons` component rendering branded buttons for Google, GitHub, and Microsoft using their official brand assets (SVG icons). Each button initiates the OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow by redirecting to `GET /auth/oauth/{provider}`. Display provider buttons in a visually grouped section below the email/password form, separated by an "or continue with" divider. Buttons must be individually focusable and labelled for screen readers.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Three buttons render for Google, GitHub, and Microsoft with correct brand colors and logos per each provider's brand guidelines
- [ ] Each button has `aria-label="Sign in with {Provider}"` and is keyboard focusable
- [ ] Clicking a button redirects to `/auth/oauth/{provider}` which initiates the server-side PKCE redirect
- [ ] A loading state is applied to the clicked button (spinner, disabled) to prevent double-clicks during redirect
- [ ] Buttons are hidden/disabled gracefully if a provider is not configured server-side (feature-flag via env variable)

---

### [E3-S2] OAuth 2.0 PKCE Backend Integration
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, oauth, security

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a secure OAuth 2.0 PKCE callback handler so that social login tokens are exchanged server-side and not exposed to the browser.

**Work Description**
Configure Passport.js with Google, GitHub, and Microsoft OAuth strategies. `GET /auth/oauth/:provider` generates a PKCE `code_verifier`/`code_challenge` pair, stores the verifier in a short-lived Redis key (`pkce:{state}`, TTL 5 min), and redirects to the provider's authorization URL. `GET /auth/oauth/:provider/callback` validates the `state` parameter, retrieves the verifier from Redis, exchanges the code for tokens, upserts the user in PostgreSQL (`oauth_accounts` table linked to `users`), creates a session in Redis, and sets the session cookie before redirecting to the app.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] PKCE `code_verifier` is 43–128 chars of `[A-Za-z0-9\-._~]`; `code_challenge` is its S256 hash; both match the provider's requirements
- [ ] `state` parameter is validated on callback; mismatched or missing state returns `400` and aborts the flow
- [ ] OAuth user upsert correctly handles: new user (create), existing email (link account), returning OAuth user (update tokens)
- [ ] Provider access tokens are never stored in PostgreSQL; only `provider_user_id` and `provider` are persisted
- [ ] All three providers (Google, GitHub, Microsoft) complete the full auth cycle in the local dev environment using test app credentials

---

### [E3-S3] MFA OTP Verification Screen
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** low | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 4
**Tags:** frontend, mfa, security

**User Story**
As a security-conscious end user, I want to enter a one-time password as a second authentication step so that my account is protected even if my password is compromised.

**Work Description**
Build the `MFAVerificationScreen` component that renders after a successful primary login when `mfa_required: true` is returned in the login response

intent.concept: A secure and user-friendly login page design that provides seamless authentication for end users. The interface will prioritize usability, accessibility, and security best practices to ensure a smooth onboarding and re-entry experience. It serves as the primary gateway to a web application or platform.
intent.problem: Many applications suffer from poorly designed login experiences that frustrate users, reduce conversion rates, and expose security vulnerabilities due to lack of proper authentication UI patterns and accessibility standards.

pain_points: Confusing or cluttered login interfaces that slow down access
Lack of clear error messaging when credentials fail
No support for password recovery or alternative login methods
Poor mobile responsiveness making login difficult on small screens
High user drop-off rates at the login stage
Security concerns due to weak authentication UI patterns
Brand inconsistency on the login page compared to the rest of the app
No standardized design system or reusable components for authentication flows
Difficulty integrating login UI with backend authentication APIs
Lack of accessibility compliance guidance for login forms
